name: Accessibility Tool IFEO Hijack
description: Attacker sets a debugger key on accessibility tools (sethc, magnify, osk, narrator) via reg.exe to gain SYSTEM shell on logon screen.

name: PowerShell Encoded Command Persistence
description: Persistence entry uses PowerShell with encoded/obfuscated command — classic fileless technique used across many APT campaigns.

name: LOLBin Chain Persistence (Script Engine to reg.exe)
description: Scripting engine spawns reg.exe or schtasks to establish persistence — common in red team frameworks and APT tooling.

name: PowerShell to schtasks Persistence
description: PowerShell spawns schtasks.exe to create a scheduled task — extremely common in Cobalt Strike post-exploitation and APT29 tradecraft.

name: cmd.exe to sc.exe Service Installation
description: cmd.exe spawns sc.exe to install a service — used for persistence and privilege escalation by multiple APT groups.

name: Hidden PowerShell Window Scheduled Task
description: Scheduled task runs PowerShell with -WindowStyle Hidden and -NonInteractive flags — classic APT staging technique for C2 beacons.

name: AppData Roaming Persistence Binary
description: Persistence binary in AppData\Roaming — standard dropper placement across many APT families to avoid requiring admin rights.

name: Temp Directory Service Binary
description: Service binary in Windows Temp directory — typical of dropper-installed backdoors and RATs.

name: Base64 Encoded Scheduled Task Payload
description: Scheduled task command contains Base64-encoded payload — used to evade command-line detection.

name: Masquerading as Windows Update
description: Entry name or binary masquerades as Windows Update infrastructure — common APT deception technique.

name: rundll32 Scheduled Task LOLBin
description: Scheduled task executes via rundll32.exe — widely used LOLBin technique.

name: Service Name Mimicry (svchost/lsass impersonation)
description: Service or task name mimics legitimate Windows process names — classic APT defense evasion.

name: Commodity Info Stealer — AppData Run Key
description: Random or generic-named Run key pointing to binary in AppData/Temp — primary persistence method of commodity MaaS info stealers. These tools steal browser cookies, saved passwords, and crypto wallets then exfiltrate to C2.

name: Browser Credential Store Reference in Persistence
description: Persistence value directly references browser profile paths (Cookies, Login Data, Web Data). This is a direct fingerprint of info stealers — legitimate software has no reason to reference these paths in a Run key.

name: Crypto Wallet Targeting Persistence
description: Persistence entry references cryptocurrency wallet paths or browser extension storage. Stealers targeting crypto holders specifically look for MetaMask, Phantom, Exodus, and similar wallet data.

name: PowerShell Download Cradle + AppData Dropper
description: PowerShell downloads and executes payload stored in AppData — standard commodity stealer distribution chain seen in fake CAPTCHA, malvertising, and cracked software campaigns.

name: Fake Installer Dropper Pattern
description: Persistence established by a process with an installer-like name from a temp or download location — common in trojanised software, cracked games, and fake tools distributed via YouTube/Discord/torrents.

name: Scheduled Task Stealer Persistence with Short Interval
description: Scheduled task with a very short repeat interval pointing to binary in user-writable directory — stealers use this to re-run and re-exfiltrate if the first run is interrupted.

name: Clipper / Crypto Hijacker Persistence
description: Persistence entry associated with clipboard monitoring for crypto address replacement. Clippers watch the clipboard for wallet addresses and silently replace them with the attacker's address.

name: Cobalt Strike Beacon Staging Pattern
description: Persistence pattern matching Cobalt Strike beacon staging: PowerShell with reflection or assembly loading from temp path — seen across dozens of APT and ransomware campaigns.

name: WMI Event Subscription Persistence
description: WMI event subscription used for persistence — nearly invisible to basic tools, survives reboots. Used heavily by APT29 (Cozy Bear) and other sophisticated actors. Triggered by __EventFilter + __CommandLineEventConsumer combination.

name: COM Object Hijacking
description: Persistence via COM object hijacking — malicious DLL registered in HKCU to override a system COM object. Executes when the hijacked COM object is invoked by a legitimate process, making it very hard to detect.

name: Startup Folder Dropper
description: Binary dropped into user or common Startup folder — simple but effective persistence used widely across APT and commodity malware families.

name: Kimsuky / North Korean Actor PowerShell Beacon
description: PowerShell persistence with noprofile + bypass + encoded command targeting C2 infrastructure — matches documented Kimsuky and Lazarus Group tradecraft used in espionage campaigns against government and crypto targets.

name: TA505 / Dridex Scheduled Task Pattern
description: Scheduled task with random or GUID-like name created by cmd or PowerShell — matches TA505 and Dridex distribution campaigns. Often used as initial persistence for ransomware pre-positioning.

name: Registry Run Key via mshta / HTA
description: mshta.exe used to write or execute persistence — HTA (HTML Application) files are a classic APT delivery and persistence mechanism that bypasses many application controls.

name: certutil Payload Decode + Execute
description: certutil.exe used to decode and execute a payload — widely abused LOLBin for stager decoding. Used in APT campaigns and commodity malware because it's a signed Microsoft binary.
